annotations.text: Did this patient have a community-acquired pneumonia? The increased frequency of community-acquired pneumonias is well recognized in patients infected with HIV.

Since  *Pneumocystis carinii* is the most common single pathogen causing pneumonia in patients with AIDS, occurring in 80 percent of such patients during the course of their illness, I have to include it in the differential diagnosis of this patient's disease
The lung is affected by one of these processes as the initial manifestation of AIDS in about 65 percent of patients infected with HIV.
Although these infections often present with atypical features, such as diffuse multilobar infiltration, the response to conventional antibiotic therapy is usually prompt.

The nonproductive cough, dyspnea, and fever, the widened arterialalveolar gradient, and the radiographic pattern of diffuse interstitial pulmonary infiltrates are all consistent with pneumonia caused by  *P. carinii.
Several features of the case, however, are inconsistent with this diagnosis.
Kaposi's sarcoma is the most frequently encountered tumor in persons with AIDS, affecting about 35 to 48 percent of them.
The frequency of lung involvement may be as high as 55 percent in patients with disseminated disease.
Because of these atypical features, tuberculosis in a patient with AIDS may easily be confused with other opportunistic infections.

Tuberculosis presenting concomitantly with another infectious complication of AIDS, such as infection with  *P. carinii* , and appearing as diffuse interstitial infiltrates on the radiographs of the chest is not uncommon
ecause this patient remained relatively well and had no evidence of a gastrointestinal disturbance, I doubt very much that his pulmonary disease was caused by the  *M. avium–intracellulare* complex.
I am finally led to consider infection with  *Histoplasma capsulatum* in this patient
Histoplasmosis has been reported in patients with AIDS from areas where this infection is endemic and from areas where it is not.
The radiographs of the chest reveal reticulonodular, diffuse infiltrates in 41 percent of the cases and alveolar infiltrates in 17 percent of the cases.
An immunodiffusion test for M and H precipitins and complement-fixation titers can be helpful in the diagnosis but may be negative.

Did this patient have progressive disseminated histoplasmosis? Although I cannot rule out miliary tuberculosis on the basis of the findings, I favor progressive disseminated histoplasmosis as the principal diagnosis in this case because of the history of recent previous residence in an area where the infection is endemic and the relatively slow evolution of the disease, with minimal respiratory tract symptoms.
